mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes-Benz Viano 2.2 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Viano 2.2 CDI (OM646) engine typically costs £2,500–£6,500 supply only. Used engines are available from specialist suppliers at competitive prices. An OM646 replacement cylinder head costs around £575. Repair costs for an OM646 engine failure in a Viano are typically between £3,300–£4,200. Fitting adds £800–£1,500 depending on workshop location. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your van.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes-Benz Viano 2.2 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646 is considered 'one of the best Merc engines out there' if properly maintained. It has a well-documented reputation for longevity — examples have run to close on 500,000 miles without major failures. However, it has predictable weak points: injector seal 'black death' is the most common issue, along with oil leaks from the oil filter housing and oil cooler, and fuel system sensor faults. With proper maintenance, the OM646 is a robust and long-lasting engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes-Benz Viano 2.2 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector seal failure ('black death') — diesel smell, hissing, carbon buildup; oil leaks from the oil filter housing and oil cooler; fuel system sensor faults causing engine warning lights and restricted RPM; common rail pressure control problems; and cold start issues from faulty camshaft or crankshaft position sensors.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes-Benz Viano 2.2 CDI engine, or should I scrap the van?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold: if your Viano is worth £4,000–£8,000 and a recon engine is £3,500–£4,500 (44–100% of the vehicle's value), assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with good bodywork justify replacement spend. Tired examples with significant rust may not. The OM646's legendary durability means a well-maintained engine can outlast the bodywork.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes-Benz Viano 2.2 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The Viano 2.2 CDI uses the OM646 — available as OM646.980 (2003–2009) or OM646.982 (2006–2014). The engine is a 2,148 cc, 16-valve DOHC turbo-diesel producing 150 PS (110 kW) and 330 Nm of torque. Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the injector seals ('black death'), or does the engine need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. Injector seal replacement (£300–£800) is significantly cheaper than a full engine replacement. If caught early, the job is straightforward — replace the copper seals and recut the seats in the head. If ignored, injectors can seize in the cylinder head, requiring specialist removal. At the first sign of diesel smell, hissing or visible carbon deposits, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz Viano 2.2 CDI Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available — specialist reconditioning is the route

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect current market rates. A used Viano 2.2 CDI engine is available from specialist suppliers at competitive prices . An OM646 replacement cylinder head costs around £575 . Repair costs for an OM646 engine failure in a Viano are typically between €3,900–€5,000 (£3,300–£4,200) . The OM646 is highly sought-after as a reconditioned unit due to its straightforward construction and parts availability .
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a Viano 2.2 CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent). The front-engine layout in the W639 Viano makes the engine relatively accessible compared to passenger cars.

cta.label: → Compare Viano 2.2 CDI engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Injector Seal Failure — "Black Death" (OM646)
problems.symptoms: Diesel smell in the cabin, hissing sounds from the engine, visible black tar-like residue on the cylinder head, rough running, hard starting, loss of power. The leaking copper seals allow combustion gases and diesel to escape, creating carbon deposits around the injectors .
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles — common on high-mileage OM646 engines .
problems.repairCost: £300–£800 (seal replacement, surface cleaning, injector seat recutting). The copper seals themselves are inexpensive, but labour is required.
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, injectors can seize in the cylinder head.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you smell diesel or hear hissing, book an inspection today.

problems.recommendation: Injector seal failure — often referred to as "black death" or Dieselkriechen — is one of the most common issues on the OM646 . If caught early, the fix is straightforward — replace the copper seals and recut the seats in the head. If ignored, the carbon buildup can become so severe that injectors become "properly welded in", requiring specialist removal. At the first sign of diesel smell, hissing or visible carbon deposits, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes with the correct MB specification help prevent injector issues .

problems.title: Oil Leaks (Oil Filter Housing & Oil Cooler)
problems.symptoms: Visible oil stains under the vehicle, burning oil smell from the engine bay, oil residue on the engine block, gradual oil level decrease. The oil filter housing and oil cooler are known weak points for leaks .
problems.typicalMileage: 60,000–120,000 miles — age-related as seals and gaskets deteriorate.
problems.repairCost: £200–£800 (oil filter housing gasket, oil cooler seals).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, oil loss can lead to low oil pressure and engine damage.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: Oil leaks from the oil filter housing and oil cooler are common on the OM646 as the engine ages . If you notice oil spots where you park or a burning oil smell, have the system inspected promptly. Replacing the affected seals is significantly cheaper than dealing with the consequences of low oil pressure or oil starvation.

problems.title: Fuel System Sensor & Common Rail Pressure Faults
problems.symptoms: Engine warning light, restricted RPM, loss of power, intermittent stalling, engine jerks or stutters at high speed . The engine may lose power suddenly after reaching operating temperature . The common rail pressure control valve can fail, triggering P2020 errors .
problems.typicalMileage: 80,000–150,000 miles — common rail system components can fail with age.
problems.repairCost: £300–£1,000 (fuel temperature sensor, common rail pressure sensor, fuel regulation valve, fuel filter).
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only; if ignored, the vehicle may become undriveable.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if symptoms appear.

problems.recommendation: The Viano 2.2 CDI often faces engine warning lights and limited acceleration due to fuel system sensor or valve faults . A faulty common rail pressure sensor or fuel regulation valve can cause restricted RPM . A defective fuel temperature sensor can cause a sudden loss of power after operating temperature is reached . If you experience any of these symptoms, have the fuel system diagnosed promptly. Check and replace the fuel filter — it may be full and sending water down the lines .

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Viano 2.2 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch early — copper seals are inexpensive
Common maintenance — repair when symptoms appear
Diagnose and replace faulty sensors or valves
Replace valve and check fuel filter
Rare but possible at very high mileage
Full engine replacement required if bottom-end or valvetrain fails

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Viano 2.2 CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full service history with regular oil changes using the correct MB specification
* No diesel smell in the cabin or hissing from the engine — injector seals intact
* No engine warning lights or fuel system faults
* Good bodywork and rust-free condition (W639 vans are prone to corrosion)
avoidIf: * Diesel smell in the cabin or hissing — injector seal failure ("black death")
* Engine warning light with restricted RPM — fuel system sensor or valve faults
* Oil leaks or burning oil smell — oil filter housing or oil cooler seals failing
* Poor service history — the OM646 is reliable but unforgiving of neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The OM646 is considered "one of the best Merc engines out there" if properly maintained . It has a well-documented reputation for longevity — examples have run to close on 500,000 miles without major failures . However, it has predictable weak points: injector seal "black death" is the most common issue, along with oil leaks from the oil filter housing and oil cooler, and fuel system sensor faults. The OM646 is a straightforward engine with good parts availability, making it a "highly sought-after replacement engine in the UK market" . With the 70% Mercedes scrap threshold, a £3,500–£4,500 reconditioned engine on a Viano valued at £4,000–£8,000 is 44–100% of the vehicle's value — assess the overall condition carefully. Well-maintained examples with good bodywork justify replacement spend. Tired examples with significant rust may not.

cta.label: → Compare Viano 2.2 CDI engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes-Benz Viano 2.2 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Diesel Smell — Any strong diesel smell in the cabin? (injector seal "black death")
* Hissing Sound — Listen for hissing from the engine (injector seal failure)
* Cold Start — Does it start easily? Hard starting can indicate sensor or injector issues
* Engine Warning Light — Any warning lights? Could indicate fuel system sensor faults
* Oil Leaks — Check around oil filter housing and oil cooler
* Service History — Regular oil changes using the correct MB specification are essential
* Rust Check — Check sills, wheel arches and chassis (W639 vans are prone to corrosion)
* MOT History — Check for corrosion and emissions-related failures
